docs.title: NLUX
docs.text: NLUX is een Collections Discovery platform voor de Nederlandse Cultureel Erfgoed sector, gebouwd op Project LUX van Yale University en de open Linked.art standaard (gebaseerd op CIDOC-CRM).

docs.title: Missie
docs.text: NLUX maakt Nederlandse erfgoedcollecties vindbaar, verbindbaar en open — voor onderzoekers, instellingen en het publiek — via gedeelde open-source infrastructuur gebouwd op internationale linked data standaarden.

docs.title: Status
docs.text:
Het project bevindt zich momenteel in Phase 0 — Foundation (Q2 2026).

Zie de Roadmap voor een volledig overzicht van mijlpalen en planning.

docs.title: Documentatie
docs.text:
• Missie en Waarden — Charter, waarden en governance
  • Roadmap — Gecombineerde tijdlijn over alle domeinen
  • Technisch Implementatieplan — Architectuur en tech stack
  • Businessplan — Financiën, partners en organisatie
  • Marketingplan — Kanalen, boodschap en lancering

docs.title: Links
docs.text:
• GitHub: nlux-project
  • Gebaseerd op: Yale LUX

docs.title: NLUX Business Plan
docs.text: Version: 0.1.0-draft Date: 2026-04-03 Status: Draft — Initial outline for funding applications and organisational participation

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
NLUX is an open-source collections discovery platform for Dutch Cultural Heritage institutions. It provides a modern, standards-based (Linked.art / CIDOC-CRM) way to explore and interconnect museum and heritage collections online. Built on Yale University's proven LUX platform, NLUX reduces the cost and technical complexity for Dutch institutions to offer world-class digital discovery to their audiences.

NLUX is organised as a not-for-profit, open-source project. Revenue is generated through implementation services, consulting, training, and grant funding — not through licensing or data lock-in. All software and data standards remain open.

docs.title: 1. Problem Statement
docs.text:
Dutch cultural heritage institutions — museums, archives, libraries — hold world-class collections. Their digital discovery infrastructure is fragmented:

  • Each institution runs a separate, siloed online collection system.
  • Cross-institutional search is either absent or limited to aggregators (Europeana, Erfgoed Leiden) that normalise data to a low common denominator.
  • Integration with global linked data ecosystems (Wikidata, Getty, VIAF) is inconsistent.
  • Small and medium institutions lack the IT capacity and budget to build modern discovery platforms.

LUX, developed by Yale University, solves exactly this problem for Yale's libraries and museums. NLUX brings this solution to the Netherlands.

docs.title: 2. Solution
docs.text:
NLUX provides:

  1. A shared platform: one installation serves multiple institutions, reducing per-institution cost.
  2. Linked data at the core: collections are described in Linked.art (CIDOC-CRM), enabling cross-institutional entity linking and integration with internationally shared authority files (AAT, Wikidata, RKD, NTA).
  3. Open source: institutions are never locked into a vendor; the code is publicly auditable and improvable.
  4. LUX compatibility: NLUX stays 100% compatible with Yale LUX, giving Dutch institutions access to a large, sustained upstream project.
  5. Dutch localisation: Dutch-language interface, Dutch authority files, and alignment with Dutch standards (NDE, DERA).

docs.title: Primary: Dutch Cultural Heritage Institutions
docs.text:
Small to medium institutions with collections online but limited digital capacity:

Institution Location Collection Focus Teylers Museum Haarlem Natural history, art, scientific instruments Museum Boerhaave Leiden History of science and medicine Huis van Hilde Castricum Archaeological finds, North Holland RMO (Rijksmuseum van Oudheden) Leiden Archaeology, ancient civilisations

Additional prospects: regional archeological services (ADC, BAAC), provincial museums, art museums outside the Randstad.

docs.title: Secondary: Aggregators and Infrastructure Providers
docs.text:
• NDE (Netwerk Digitaal Erfgoed): national digital heritage network; NLUX contributes to their linked data ambitions.
  • KBNL (Koninklijke Bibliotheek): national library; potential data contributor and partner.
  • Europeana: NLUX can feed standardised Linked.art data into Europeana.

docs.title: 4. Revenue Model
docs.text: NLUX operates as a not-for-profit project. Revenue streams:

docs.title: 4.1 Implementation Services (Primary)
docs.text:
Institutions pay for: - Data mapping and migration from Adlib / Axiell EMu / TMS to Linked.art - Custom connectors for their collection management systems - Deployment and integration with existing IT infrastructure - Staff training

Estimated rate: €900–1,500/day consulting; €15,000–60,000 per institution onboarding project.

docs.title: 4.2 Hosting / Managed Service
docs.text: Optionally, NLUX can offer a managed hosting service: - Monthly fee per institution: €300–800/month (small–medium). - Covers infrastructure, monitoring, updates, and support.

docs.title: 4.3 Grants and Public Funding
docs.text:
Dutch and European funding programmes aligned with NLUX's mission:

Funder Programme Notes NWO SSH Open Science Research data / open infrastructure Creative Industries Fund NL Digital culture UX & design aspects RCE / OCW Erfgoedprogramma's Cultural heritage digitisation EU Horizon Europe Research Infrastructures Cross-border linked data EU Digital Europe Cultural heritage Digital transformation SURF SURF Open Innovation Lab Dutch research infrastructure Dutch Postcode Lottery Cultural heritage Social impact focus Private foundations Van den Broek, Prins Bernhard Cultuurfonds Heritage-specific

Target per grant cycle: €50,000–250,000.

docs.title: 4.4 Sponsorship
docs.text: Corporate sponsors (e.g. technology companies with a heritage / open source interest) can sponsor development of specific features or infrastructure.

docs.title: 4.5 Talks, Training, and Publications
docs.text: Conference talks (DH, Museums & Web, Linked Data events), workshops, and published guides generate income and visibility.

docs.title: One-Time / Setup Costs (Year 1)
docs.text: Item Estimated Cost Legal setup (foundation / stichting) €500–1,500 Accountant / notary €1,000–2,000 Domain, branding, design €2,000–5,000 Initial infrastructure setup €2,000–5,000 Travel (stakeholder meetings) €2,000–4,000 Total setup €7,500–17,500

docs.title: Recurring Costs (Annual)
docs.text: Item Estimated Cost/Year Infrastructure (hosting, CI/CD) €3,000–8,000 MarkLogic licence (if used) €0 (Developer) – €10,000+ (negotiated) Accountant / legal €2,000–4,000 Insurance €500–1,000 Travel / events €3,000–6,000 Total recurring €8,500–29,000

docs.title: Personnel
docs.text:
Initially the project is maintained by a single person (founder / lead). Target state within 2 years: - 1 FTE lead developer / project manager (founder) - 0.5 FTE data engineer (grant-funded or institutional secondment) - Community contributors (volunteer)

Break-even consulting revenue needed (Year 1): approximately €40,000–60,000 gross, assuming low overhead and partial grant funding.

docs.title: Recommended Form: Stichting (Foundation)
docs.text:
A Dutch Stichting (foundation) is the standard legal form for non-profit open source and cultural projects in the Netherlands:

  • No shareholders; profits are reinvested in the mission.
  • Can receive grants and donations.
  • Can employ staff and enter into contracts.
  • Board (bestuur) of minimum 1 person; recommended 3–5 for governance and credibility.
  • Low setup cost (~€500–1,000 notary fee).

docs.title: Governance
docs.text:
• Board: founder + 2–4 external members (heritage sector, technology, legal/finance).
  • Advisory board / user group: representatives from pilot institutions.
  • Open source project governance: contribution guidelines, code of conduct, RFC process for major changes.

docs.title: Intellectual Property
docs.text:
• All NLUX-produced source code: Apache License 2.0.
  • Trademarks (name, logo): held by the Stichting.
  • Data contributed by institutions: remains the property of the institution; licenced to NLUX for aggregation and display.

docs.title: Contracts with Institutions
docs.text:
• Memoranda of Understanding (MOUs) for pilot phase (no payment required).
  • Service agreements for production onboarding and hosting.
  • Data sharing agreements specifying licence, attribution, and withdrawal rights.

docs.title: 7. Partnerships
docs.text: Partner Role Yale / Project LUX Upstream codebase; reference implementation NDE (Netwerk Digitaal Erfgoed) Alignment with Dutch digital heritage strategy DANS / SURF Hosting and research infrastructure RKD (Netherlands Institute for Art History) Authority file integration (RKDartists) KB Nationale Bibliotheek NTA authority file; potential pilot partner Linked.art community Data model governance Europeana Data aggregation and visibility

docs.title: 8. Competitive Landscape
docs.text:
Platform Organisation Notes LUX Yale University Upstream; NLUX is built on this CollectiveAccess Open source community Collection management, not discovery Omeka Roy Rosenzweig Center Publishing, not full CMS Ibexa / Civica Commercial Vendor lock-in, expensive Europeana EU Aggregator, low metadata granularity IIIF viewers Various Viewer layer only, not search/discovery

NLUX occupies a unique position: open source, LUX-compatible, Dutch-localised, and offering a full discovery experience (not just a viewer or aggregator).

docs.title: 9. Key Milestones (Business)
docs.text: Milestone Target Date Notes Stichting incorporated Month 3 Required for grant applications First MOU signed Month 6 Teylers or Boerhaave as pilot First grant application submitted Month 4 NDE or Creative Industries Fund First grant awarded Month 9–12 Dependent on funder cycle First paying client Month 9–12 Implementation services Break-even Month 18–24 Mix of services + grants

docs.title: 10. Risks
docs.text: Risk Mitigation Too dependent on single founder Recruit board members and community early Grant application rejected Multiple parallel applications; low fixed costs MarkLogic licensing cost Open stack alternative in parallel roadmap Institutions slow to commit Start with MOUs; reduce barrier to entry LUX upstream goes closed Apache licence protects existing forks; open stack fallback GDPR / legal complexity Legal advisory board member; per-institution review

docs.title: 11. Three-Year Financial Projection (Rough)
docs.text:
Year 1 Year 2 Year 3 Implementation revenue €20,000 €60,000 €120,000 Hosting / managed service €0 €10,000 €25,000 Grants €30,000 €80,000 €100,000 Sponsorship €5,000 €10,000 €20,000 Talks / training €5,000 €10,000 €15,000 Total revenue €60,000 €170,000 €280,000 Operating costs €25,000 €50,000 €80,000 Personnel €40,000 €90,000 €140,000 Net -€5,000 €30,000 €60,000

Note: Year 1 assumes founder draws partial salary; grant funding bridges shortfall.

End of Business Plan v0.1.0-draft

docs.title: NLUX Marketing Plan — Initial Outline
docs.text: Version: 0.1.0-draft Date: 2026-04-03 Status: Draft

docs.title: 1. Positioning
docs.text:
NLUX is the open, linked-data collections discovery platform for Dutch cultural heritage. Built on world-class technology (Yale LUX), adapted for Dutch institutions, with no vendor lock-in.

Tagline (working): "Connected collections. Open by design."

docs.title: 2. Target Audiences
docs.text: Audience Their Need What NLUX Offers Collection managers (Teylers, Boerhaave, etc.) Better findability, less IT burden Managed open platform, easy onboarding IT / digital teams at institutions Interoperability, standards compliance Linked.art, open APIs, LUX compatibility Grant officers / directors Align with NDE / Europeana strategy DERA/NDE alignment, clear impact story Researchers / Digital Humanists Machine-readable, linked collections data Open APIs, SPARQL endpoint, Linked.art data General public Discover Dutch heritage online Beautiful, fast, multilingual discovery UI

docs.title: 3. Key Messages
docs.text:
1. Open and free: No licence fees. Code is Apache 2.0. Your data stays yours.
  2. World-class technology: Built on Yale LUX — proven at scale in one of the world's great universities.
  3. Linked data without the pain: Full Linked.art / CIDOC-CRM compliance, with Dutch authority files integrated out of the box.
  4. Small team, big impact: Affordable for medium and small institutions that cannot build this themselves.
  5. Community, not a vendor: A foundation, not a company. Governed by its users.

docs.title: 4.1 Community and Sector Networks
docs.text:
• NDE (Netwerk Digitaal Erfgoed) events and working groups
  • DEN (Digitaal Erfgoed Nederland) community forums
  • FARO (Flemish heritage network) — future cross-border expansion
  • Linked.art community calls and GitHub discussions
  • Museums Vereniging and sector associations

docs.title: 4.2 Conferences and Events
docs.text:
• Museums & Web (international)
  • DH Benelux
  • CIDOC annual conference
  • Europeana Tech
  • Open Science NL

docs.title: 4.3 Digital Presence
docs.text:
• Project website (GitHub Pages initially): nlux-project.org or nlux.nl
  • GitHub organisation: nlux-project
  • Mastodon / LinkedIn for project updates (no Twitter/X)
  • Monthly newsletter (Buttondown or similar, free tier)

docs.title: 4.4 Publications and Outreach
docs.text:
• Blog posts on implementation experiences, Linked.art tips
  • Case studies per pilot institution
  • Technical papers for DH / GLAM conferences

docs.title: Phase 0 — Stealth / Build (Months 1–6)
docs.text:
• Engage pilot institutions in private conversations.
  • Build proof-of-concept with Teylers data.
  • Prepare website and GitHub organisation.

docs.title: Phase 1 — Soft Launch (Month 6–9)
docs.text:
• Announce at one NDE event or working group.
  • Publish GitHub organisation and documentation site.
  • Release pilot demo URL.
  • Targeted outreach to 5–10 institutions.

docs.title: Phase 2 — Public Launch (Month 10–12)
docs.text:
• Press release / blog post on project website.
  • Submit talk proposals to Museums & Web and DH Benelux.
  • Launch newsletter.
  • Report on pilot results.

docs.title: 6. Success Metrics (Year 1)
docs.text:
Metric Target Institutions in conversation 10 MOUs signed 2–3 GitHub stars 50+ Newsletter subscribers 200+ Conference presentations 2 Grant applications submitted 3

End of Marketing Plan v0.1.0-draft

docs.title: NLUX — Mission, Values, and Charter
docs.text: Version: 0.1.0-draft Date: 2026-04-03

docs.title: Mission Statement
docs.text: NLUX makes Dutch cultural heritage collections discoverable, connectable, and open — for researchers, institutions, and the public — through shared open-source infrastructure built on international linked data standards.

docs.title: Vision
docs.text: A Netherlands where every heritage institution, regardless of size or budget, can participate in a shared, interoperable digital discovery landscape: one where a medieval manuscript in Leiden, a scientific instrument in Haarlem, and an archaeological find in Castricum can all be found, compared, and understood in their full context.

docs.title: 1. Open by Default
docs.text: All code is open source (Apache 2.0). All data standards are open (Linked.art, CIDOC-CRM). We do not build walls around knowledge.

docs.title: 2. Community over Commerce
docs.text: NLUX is a foundation, not a product company. Decisions are made in the interest of the cultural heritage community, not shareholders or a single vendor.

docs.title: 3. Standards First
docs.text: We implement international standards (Linked.art, CIDOC-CRM, IIIF, SPARQL) faithfully. We contribute improvements upstream. We do not invent proprietary formats when open ones exist.

docs.title: 4. Sustainability
docs.text: We design for the long term: low dependencies, documented everything, upstream compatibility. A project that outlives its founder.

docs.title: 5. Accessibility and Inclusion
docs.text: Our platform should be usable by institutions with limited IT resources, developers without linked data backgrounds, and end users speaking Dutch, English, or other languages.

docs.title: 6. Transparency
docs.text: Finances, roadmaps, decisions, and discussions are public. We acknowledge our limitations and uncertainties.

docs.title: Name
docs.text: The project is called NLUX (working name; formally: NLUX – Nederlandse Linked Erfgoed Uitwisseling or similar, to be confirmed at incorporation).

docs.title: Legal Form
docs.text: A Dutch Stichting (foundation), incorporated under Dutch law, with a registered office in the Netherlands.

docs.title: Purpose (Statutair Doel)
docs.text:
The foundation's statutory purpose is:

To develop, maintain, and promote open-source software and open data standards that enable Dutch cultural heritage institutions to share and discover their collections online, and to do everything related to or conducive to this purpose.

docs.title: Membership and Governance
docs.text:
• The Stichting is governed by a Board (bestuur) of 3–7 members.
  • The Board includes a chair, treasurer, and secretary.
  • An informal User Group (vertegenwoordiging van deelnemende instellingen) advises the Board.
  • Major decisions (licence changes, mergers, dissolution) require a supermajority (2/3) of the Board.

docs.title: Intellectual Property Policy
docs.text:
• Code produced by NLUX is published under Apache License 2.0.
  • The NLUX name and logo are trademarks of the Stichting.
  • Data contributed by institutions is not transferred to NLUX; institutions grant a licence for aggregation and display.

docs.title: Code of Conduct
docs.text: NLUX adopts the Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct for all community spaces (GitHub, mailing lists, events).

docs.title: Dissolution
docs.text:
In the event of dissolution, any remaining assets are transferred to an organisation with a compatible mission (e.g. NDE, a comparable open cultural heritage foundation).

End of Mission, Values, and Charter v0.1.0-draft

docs.title: NLUX Unified Roadmap
docs.text:
Version: 0.2.0-draft Date: 2026-04-04 Status: Draft

This document combines milestones from the Technical Implementation Plan, Business Plan, and Marketing Plan into a single timeline. Each milestone is tagged with its domain:

  • [TECH] — Technical Implementation Plan
  • [BIZ] — Business Plan
  • [MKT] — Marketing Plan
  • [LEGAL] — Legal / Governance

docs.title: Phase 0 — Foundation (Months 1–3, target: Q2 2026)
docs.text:
Goal: establish the project's legal, organisational, and technical foundation.

# Milestone Domain Notes 0.1 Register GitHub organisation (nlux-project) [TECH] Fork LUX repos; set up upstream tracking branches 0.2 Build nlux-backend (FastAPI/SQLite/PostgreSQL) [TECH] Replaces MarkLogic; compatible with lux-frontend API — done 2026-04-04 0.3 Deploy lux-frontend + nlux-backend locally (Docker) [TECH] Validate full stack runs end-to-end 0.4b Ingest Teylers Museum sample data into nlux-backend [TECH] First real Dutch dataset via load_data.py 0.4 Set up NLUX documentation site (this MkDocs repo) [TECH] Published to GitHub Pages 0.5 Define contribution guidelines and code of conduct [TECH] [LEGAL] Contributor Covenant; CONTRIBUTING.md 0.6 Incorporate Stichting NLUX with notary [LEGAL] [BIZ] Required before grant applications 0.7 Appoint initial board (3 members minimum) [LEGAL] [BIZ] Recruit 2 external board members 0.8 Open private conversations with Teylers and Boerhaave [BIZ] [MKT] No commitment required; gauge interest 0.9 Register domain (nlux.nl or nlux-project.org) [MKT] DNS, basic landing page 0.10 ~~Agree on MarkLogic strategy~~ → decided: nlux-backend [TECH] [BIZ] MarkLogic ruled out; FastAPI/PostgreSQL backend built — done 2026-04-04

Phase 0 exit criteria: Stichting incorporated; LUX running locally with Dutch sample data; at least 2 institutions in conversation.

docs.title: Phase 1 — Pilot (Months 4–9, target: Q3–Q4 2026)
docs.text:
Goal: visible proof-of-concept, first formal partners, first grant application.

# Milestone Domain Notes 1.1 Dutch (nl) i18n for LUX frontend [TECH] Adds Dutch locale; no structural changes 1.2 Minimal NLUX branding (logo, colours) via config [TECH] Environment variables / theme config only 1.3 Data pipeline for Teylers Museum (Adlib/Axiell → Linked.art) [TECH] Custom connector; published as open source 1.4 Staging environment (GitHub Pages + hosted backend) [TECH] Publicly accessible demo URL 1.5 Security and GDPR review of staging environment [TECH] [LEGAL] Before sharing with institutions 1.6 Sign first MOU (Teylers or Boerhaave) [BIZ] [LEGAL] Template MOU; no payment required at pilot stage 1.7 Submit first grant application (NDE or Creative Industries Fund NL) [BIZ] Stichting required; aim for ≥€30,000 1.8 Soft launch: announce at NDE event or working group [MKT] Present demo; publish GitHub org and docs site 1.9 Collect feedback from pilot institutions [TECH] [BIZ] Structured interviews; feed into Phase 2 backlog 1.10 Publish first case study (Teylers pilot) [MKT] Blog post + documentation page 1.11 Open source release: tag v0.1.0 on all NLUX repos [TECH] Semantic versioning begins

Phase 1 exit criteria: Live demo with real institutional data; at least 1 MOU signed; 1 grant application submitted; public GitHub organisation visible.

docs.title: Phase 2 — Production (Months 10–18, target: Q1–Q3 2027)
docs.text:
Goal: first production deployment, paying clients, first grant award.

# Milestone Domain Notes 2.1 Production infrastructure (SURF or Hetzner) [TECH] Replaces staging; SLA-backed 2.2 Formal onboarding of pilot institutions (service agreements) [BIZ] [LEGAL] First recurring revenue 2.3 Data pipelines for 2–3 institutions [TECH] Reuse and extend Teylers connector 2.4 OpenSearch backend evaluation [TECH] Parallel to MarkLogic; compare search quality 2.5 Public launch [MKT] Press release; institution pages live 2.6 First grant awarded [BIZ] Fund data engineer (0.5 FTE) 2.7 Submit talk proposals (Museums & Web, DH Benelux) [MKT] Abstract submission deadlines vary 2.8 Launch newsletter [MKT] Target 200 subscribers by end of phase 2.9 Recruit first community contributor [TECH] Open issues labelled good first issue 2.10 Tag v1.0.0 — first production release [TECH] Changelog; migration guide from v0.x 2.11 User group / steering group first meeting [LEGAL] [BIZ] Formalise institutional participation

Phase 2 exit criteria: Production deployment live; ≥2 paying institutions; first grant funding received; v1.0.0 tagged.

docs.title: Phase 3 — Growth (Months 19–36, target: Q4 2027–Q2 2028)
docs.text:
Goal: sustainable operation, community growth, cross-institutional entity linking.

# Milestone Domain Notes 3.1 Onboard 3–5 additional institutions [BIZ] [TECH] Standardised onboarding playbook 3.2 Cross-institutional entity linking (persons, objects, places) [TECH] Wikidata / RKD reconciliation pipeline 3.3 Public SPARQL endpoint [TECH] For researchers and third-party apps 3.4 OpenSearch backend production migration (if validated) [TECH] Removes MarkLogic dependency 3.5 Second major grant application (EU Horizon / Digital Europe) [BIZ] Requires track record and partners 3.6 Bilateral partnership with Flemish / Belgian institution [BIZ] [MKT] FARO or a Flemish museum 3.7 Annual NLUX community event [MKT] Workshop day; hybrid format 3.8 Financial sustainability review [BIZ] Board reviews 3-year projection; adjust strategy 3.9 Contribution back to Yale LUX upstream [TECH] PRs, bug reports, or feature proposals

Phase 3 exit criteria: Organisation financially self-sustaining (no dependency on single grant); ≥5 active institutions; active community of contributors.

docs.title: Dependencies and Critical Path
docs.text:
0.6 Stichting incorporated
  └── 1.6 First MOU
  └── 1.7 First grant application
        └── 2.6 First grant awarded
              └── 3.5 EU grant application

0.2 LUX running locally
  └── 0.3 Teylers sample data
        └── 1.3 Teylers pipeline
              └── 1.4 Staging environment
                    └── 2.1 Production infrastructure

0.2 nlux-backend built (FastAPI/SQLite/PostgreSQL)
  └── 0.3 Deploy lux-frontend + nlux-backend locally
        └── 0.4b Ingest Teylers sample data
              └── 1.4 Staging environment
                    └── 2.4 OpenSearch evaluation (optional)
                          └── 3.4 OpenSearch migration (optional)

docs.title: Key Risks by Phase
docs.text: Phase Top Risk Mitigation 0 Stichting setup delayed (notary availability) Start process immediately; use template statuten 1 Institutions not ready to sign an MOU Lower barrier: informal letter of intent first 2 Grant application rejected Multiple parallel applications; NDE + Creative Industries 2 nlux-backend search quality at scale Evaluate OpenSearch as Phase 2 backend if PostgreSQL FTS insufficient 3 Single founder burnout Recruit board and contributors before Phase 3 begins

docs.title: Document Cross-References
docs.text:
Topic Detail in Architecture & tech stack NLUX-technical-implementation-plan.md Revenue, costs, projections NLUX-business-plan.md Channels, messaging, launch NLUX-marketing-plan.md Mission, values, governance NLUX-mission-values.md

End of Unified Roadmap v0.1.0-draft

docs.title: NLUX Technical Implementation Plan
docs.text: Version: 0.2.0-draft Date: 2026-04-04 Status: Draft

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: NLUX (Nederlandse Linked Data Uitwisseling / NL Collections Discovery) is a collections discovery platform for the Dutch Cultural Heritage sector. It is built on top of Yale University's LUX: Yale Collections Discovery platform and reuses as much of LUX's codebase, tooling, and architecture as possible. NLUX must remain 100% compatible with Project LUX — i.e., any LUX dataset should work in NLUX without modification, and vice versa.

docs.title: 2. LUX Architecture Overview
docs.text:
Understanding the upstream LUX platform is the prerequisite for NLUX. LUX consists of:

Component Technology Repository Data pipeline Python, XSLT, shell project-lux/lux-data Backend search MarkLogic (NoSQL XML/JSON) project-lux/lux-backend Frontend React / TypeScript project-lux/lux-frontend Documentation MkDocs / Linked.art project-lux/documentation Data model Linked.art (CIDOC-CRM) —

NLUX will fork or extend each component, applying minimal Dutch-specific customisations.

docs.title: 3. Goals and Constraints
docs.text:
• 100% LUX compatibility: data model stays Linked.art / CIDOC-CRM; APIs remain LUX-compatible.
  • Minimal customisation: prefer configuration over code changes; maintain a thin diff from upstream.
  • Open source: all NLUX-specific code is published under an OSI-approved licence (Apache 2.0 recommended).
  • Dutch Cultural Heritage focus: initial target institutions include Teylers Museum, Museum Boerhaave, Huis van Hilde, RMO Leiden.
  • Low cost: avoid or minimise proprietary/commercial dependencies (especially MarkLogic licensing).

docs.title: 4. Architecture Decision: Backend — Decision Record
docs.text: LUX uses MarkLogic as its backend, which is a commercial product. After evaluation, MarkLogic has been ruled out for NLUX (see nlux-project/nlux#10).

docs.title: Decision (2026-04-04): nlux-backend
docs.text:
MarkLogic was found to be too complex and overengineered for NLUX's Phase 0 needs. NLUX will use a purpose-built open-source backend:

nlux-backend — a simplified Python/FastAPI service that: - Implements the LUX middletier REST API contract (compatible with lux-frontend and lux-middletier) - Stores and serves Linked Art JSON-LD records - Uses SQLite in development and PostgreSQL in production - Is fully containerised with Docker

docs.title: Rejected options
docs.text: Option Reason rejected MarkLogic Developer licence Too complex; commercial vendor lock-in; overengineered for initial scope OpenSearch + triplestore Significant engineering effort deferred to Phase 2+

docs.title: Future
docs.text: An OpenSearch evaluation track remains open for Phase 2 if PostgreSQL full-text search proves insufficient at scale.

docs.title: 5.1 Data Model
docs.text:
• Adopt Linked.art in full, as documented at project-lux.github.io/documentation.
  • Extend with Dutch-specific vocabularies where needed (e.g. RKDartists, NTA authority files, AAT Dutch labels), always as additions that do not break the core Linked.art shape.
  • Use the NLUX documentation site (this MkDocs repo) to document Dutch extensions.

docs.title: 5.2 Data Pipeline
docs.text:
• Fork project-lux/lux-data.
  • Add Dutch institution connectors:
  • RijksmuseuM API (already JSON-LD / Linked.art friendly)
  • Adlib / Axiell EMu XML exports (Teylers, RMO, etc.)
  • OAI-PMH endpoints
  • Transformation tooling: Python + XSLT (as upstream); consider adding a CoGS pipeline step.
  • Output: Linked.art JSON-LD blobs, identical in shape to LUX production data.

docs.title: 5.3 Backend
docs.text:
nlux-backend (repository: nlux-project/nlux-backend) is NLUX's custom backend, replacing lux-marklogic.

Phase 0 (current): Core API — record retrieval, full-text search, health endpoint.

Endpoint Description GET /health Liveness check GET /data/{uri} Retrieve a Linked Art record by URI GET /api/search/{scope} Full-text search with pagination

Stack:

Layer Technology Framework FastAPI (Python 3.12) ORM SQLAlchemy 2 Database (dev) SQLite with FTS5 Database (prod) PostgreSQL with tsvector Container Docker / Docker Compose

Phase 2: Evaluate OpenSearch as a search backend if PostgreSQL FTS proves insufficient at scale.

docs.title: 5.4 Frontend
docs.text:
• Fork project-lux/lux-frontend.
  • Dutch-language translations (i18n): add nl locale using LUX's existing i18n mechanism.
  • Branding: configure theme colours, logo, and institution list via environment variables / config (no code changes).
  • Host: static build deployed to GitHub Pages (initial) → CDN (production).

docs.title: 5.5 Infrastructure
docs.text:
Environment Platform Cost Development Local Docker Compose Free Staging GitHub Actions CI + GitHub Pages Free Production (v1) A cloud provider (Hetzner, AWS, or SURF) €50–300/mo estimated MarkLogic (if Option A) MarkLogic Developer (local) or ML Cloud Free–$500+/mo

SURF Research Cloud or SURF hosted services are a strong candidate for Dutch academic/cultural heritage projects — explore eligibility.

docs.title: 5.6 DevOps & CI/CD
docs.text:
• Git monorepo or multi-repo (mirror LUX's structure).
  • GitHub Actions for CI: lint, test, build, deploy.
  • Docker Compose for local development of all services.
  • Semantic versioning; release notes per component tied to LUX upstream tags.

docs.title: 6. LUX Upstream Compatibility Strategy
docs.text:
To stay compatible with LUX:

  1. Track LUX upstream releases with a dedicated branch (e.g. upstream/main).
  2. Apply NLUX patches as a thin layer via git cherry-pick or patch files.
  3. Run LUX's own test suite against NLUX builds in CI.
  4. Participate in the LUX open source community (issues, PRs) where problems affect both.

docs.title: 7. Data Governance & Standards
docs.text:
• All data published as Linked.art JSON-LD, licenced CC0 or CC-BY where institutions permit.
  • Personal data (provenance, collector names): follow AVG/GDPR; no personal data in public endpoints without consent.
  • Authority files: align with Wikidata, AAT, RKD, NTA, and VIAF for entity reconciliation.
  • Persistent identifiers: use ARK or Handle URIs for NLUX-minted records; institutions retain their own PIDs.

docs.title: 8. Technology Stack Summary
docs.text: Layer Technology Data model Linked.art (JSON-LD, CIDOC-CRM) Data pipeline Python 3.11+, XSLT 2.0, Apache Jena Backend (phase 0–1) FastAPI + SQLite / PostgreSQL (nlux-backend) Backend (phase 2+) OpenSearch evaluation (optional) Frontend React 18, TypeScript, LUX frontend fork Documentation MkDocs + Material theme CI/CD GitHub Actions Container Docker / Docker Compose Hosting GitHub Pages (static), SURF or Hetzner (backend) Licence Apache 2.0 (code), CC0/CC-BY (data)

docs.title: Phase 0 — Foundation (Months 1–3)
docs.text:
• [ ] Set up GitHub organisation (e.g. nlux-project)
  • [ ] Fork LUX repositories; establish upstream tracking branch
  • [x] Build nlux-backend (FastAPI + SQLite/PostgreSQL) — replaces MarkLogic (2026-04-04)
  • [ ] Deploy lux-frontend + nlux-backend locally with Docker
  • [ ] Ingest one Dutch institution's sample data (e.g. Teylers)
  • [x] Set up NLUX documentation site (this repo)
  • [ ] Define governance and contribution guidelines

docs.title: Phase 1 — Pilot (Months 4–9)
docs.text:
• [ ] Dutch i18n for the frontend
  • [ ] Minimal branding customisation
  • [ ] Data pipeline for 2–3 pilot institutions
  • [ ] Staging environment (GitHub Pages frontend + hosted backend)
  • [ ] Demo for pilot institutions; collect feedback
  • [ ] Apply for first funding round (NWO, Creative Industries Fund NL, or EU)

docs.title: Phase 2 — Production (Months 10–18)
docs.text:
• [ ] Production infrastructure (SURF or Hetzner)
  • [ ] Onboard pilot institutions formally (MOUs / data agreements)
  • [ ] OpenSearch backend evaluation
  • [ ] Public launch
  • [ ] Submit to Dutch Open Science / GLAM networks

docs.title: Phase 3 — Growth (18+ Months)
docs.text:
• [ ] Additional institutions
  • [ ] Federated search / cross-institutional entity linking
  • [ ] API for third-party applications
  • [ ] Community governance (steering group, working groups)

docs.title: 10. Risks and Mitigations
docs.text: Risk Likelihood Mitigation MarkLogic licence cost Resolved Replaced by nlux-backend (FastAPI/PostgreSQL); MarkLogic not used LUX upstream divergence Medium Upstream tracking branch; active upstream participation Institution data quality High Data cleaning pipeline; incremental onboarding Funding gaps Medium Multiple funding sources; low fixed costs GDPR compliance Medium Legal review per institution; lean on existing policies Single maintainer dependency High Document everything; recruit volunteer contributors early

docs.title: 11. Open Source & Licensing
docs.text:
• NLUX code: Apache License 2.0
  • LUX upstream code: check each repo's licence (currently varies; some are Apache 2.0)
  • Data contributed by institutions: institution defines licence; NLUX recommends CC0 or CC-BY
  • Documentation: CC-BY 4.0

All forks and NLUX-specific code will be published under the nlux-project GitHub organisation.

End of Technical Implementation Plan v0.1.0-draft
